abstract.para: To perform maintenance tasks on the cluster nodes, you might need to stop the resources running on that node, to move them, or to shut down or reboot the node. It might also be necessary to temporarily take over the control of resources from the cluster, or even to stop the cluster service while resources remain running. This chapter explains how to manually take down a cluster node without negative side-effects. It also gives an overview of different options the cluster stack provides for executing maintenance tasks. If you don't protect the cluster before performing maintenance tasks, you risk unwanted side effects such as resources not starting in an orderly fashion, unsynchronized CIBs across the cluster nodes, or even data loss.

section.title: General workflow
section.para: To do testing or maintenance work on the cluster, nodes or individual resources, follow these general steps:

step.para: Determine the workflow you need, depending on your maintenance tasks.
Apply one or more administrative modes, depending on the workflow.
Perform your maintenance tasks or tests on the cluster, nodes or individual resources.
Put the cluster, node or resource back into normal operation.

section.title: Administrative modes
section.para: &sleha; has the following modes for protecting the cluster during maintenance tasks. Depending on your task, you might need to use one of these modes or a combination of multiple modes.

listitem.para: Stopping the cluster services means stopping &pace; and &corosync;. In this mode, the cluster is no longer running, either entirely or just on one node. Use this mode when you need to manage infrastructure, such as storage, networking or hardware.

For the whole cluster: Stopping the cluster services on all nodes at once allows you to shut down a cluster while avoiding the mass migration of resources that would happen if you shut down each node one by one. When this mode is used in combination with maintenance mode, the resources continue to run but have no cluster protection. When this mode is used on its own, all resources are stopped. However, this doesn't guarantee a graceful shutdown—if stopping a resource fails or times out, the node is fenced.

For one node: Stopping the cluster services on just one node allows the resources to move to another node. If no other node is eligible to run a resource, the resource stops. However, if stopping a resource fails or times out, the node is fenced. Therefore, we recommend using this mode in combination with standby mode or maintenance mode.

In maintenance mode, the cluster stops managing the resources and won't start, stop or monitor them. Use this mode when you need to manually manage applications or services without interference from the cluster. However, be aware that the resources won't have cluster protection while in maintenance mode.
For the whole cluster: Cluster maintenance mode puts all the resources into maintenance mode at once.
For one node: Node maintenance mode puts all the resources that are running on a specific node into maintenance mode at once.
For one resource: Resource maintenance mode puts a single resource into maintenance mode. You can also use this mode for resource groups and clones.

When a node is in standby mode, it can't run resources. The resources move to another node. If no other node is eligible to run a resource, the resource stops. This mode is useful for gracefully evacuating a node before stopping the cluster services.

When a resource is in unmanaged mode, the cluster won't start or stop it. However, unlike maintenance mode, the cluster still monitors the resource. Use this mode when you need to manually manage an application or service, but still want the cluster to report any failures.
